Facts.text: General Vespasian's troops occupy Rome after defeating the Emperor Vitellius
Pope Gregory VI abdicates at the Council of Sutri, ending a conflict between himself, Benedict IX and Sylvester III; Clement II becomes the new Pope
Pope Nicholas V appoints Rudolf of Diepholt, Bishop of Utrecht, as cardinal
English fleet & Robert Dudley, Earl of Leicester, reach Vlissingen
Ottario Rinuccini and Giulio Caccini's opera "Euridice" published
The English colonial expedition to America, consisting of the ships Discovery, Godspeed and Susan Constant, departs Blackwall, London to found Jamestown, Virginia
Corporation Act enforced in England
1st jury trial in Delaware; Marcus Jacobson condemned for insurrection & sentenced to flogging, branding & slavery
Britain declares war on Holland
French flag lowered in New Orleans to mark the formal transfer of the Louisiana Purchase from France to USA, purchased for $15M
Missouri imposes a $1 bachelor tax on unmarried men aged between 21 & 50
Great Britain, France, Prussia, Austria and Russia recognize Belgium
Hawaiian post office established

South Carolina General Assembly votes 169-0 to secede from the United States, declaring itself an "independent commonwealth". Is quickly followed by other Southern states triggering the American Civil War.
Battle of Dranesville, VA
-Dec 20th] Battle of Kelly's Ford, VA
-Jan 3rd] Vicksburg campaign
Confederate raid on Union supplies at Holly Springs, Mississippi
-Dec 27th] Battle of Ft Fisher, NC
De Clear-Alkmaar railway opens
Battle at Bronker's Spruit, Transvaal: Farmers beat Britten
NY's Broadway lit by electricity, becomes known as "Great White Way"
International cantilever railway bridge opens at Niagara Falls between Canada and US
Strongman Louis Cyr withstands pull of 4 horses
Pneumatic automobile tire patented, Syracuse, NY
1st state anti-lynching statute approved, in Georgia
England beat Australia by 10 runs in the first six-day cricket Test, Australia needed 177 to win but were all out for 166
Giacobini discovers a comet (will be 1st comet visited by spacecraft)
Venezuela (under vice-pres Gomez) attacks Dutch fleet
Explosion at Yolande Alabama, coal mine kills 91
J Hartley Manners' "Peg O' My Heart" premieres in NYC
Paul Claudels "L'Annonce Faite à Marie" premieres in Paris
Russian troops overrun Qom, Persia
A second nationwide referendum on military conscription is rejected by the Australian public
The Allies turn their attention to Bolshevik expansion in the East, landing troops in Crimea and Latvia
Canadian National Railways established (N America's longest, 50,000 KM)
US House of Representatives restricts immigration
Bert Collins scores 104 on Test Cricket debut v England SCG
Draft declaration on creating the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR) crafted by representatives from the Russian SFSR, the Ukrainian SSR, Byelorussian SSR, and the Transcaucasian SFSR
Polish parliament selects Stanislaw Wojcieckowski as president
Sidney Howard's "Silver Cord" premieres in NYC
1st international dogsled mail leaves Minot, Maine for Montreal, Quebec
Ethel Barrymore Theatre opens at 243 W 47th St NYC
Heinie Wagner replaces Bill Carrigan as Red Sox manager
Mount Davidson dedicated as a San Francisco city park
Cricketer Learie Constantine scores 100 runs in 52 mins West Indies v Tasmania (10x4, 1x6, 1x5)
Bolivia & Paraguay sign cease fire
Boston Bruins rookie goalie Frankie Brimsek records his 3rd straight shutout with a 3-0 win over the New York Americans, making it his incredible sixth shutout in his first 8 NHL games
Radio Australia begins overseas shortwave service
Free France under adm Muselier occupies St-Pierre et Miquelon
Japanese troops land on Mindanao, Philippines
World War II: First battle of the American Volunteer Group, better known as the "Flying Tigers" in Kunming, China.
1st Japanese bombing of Calcutta
"Internationale" is no longer used as USSR National Anthem
Archbishop De Young & bishop Huibers condemn black market
Battle of Bastogne: Nazis surround 101st Airborne (NUTS!)
Bishop forbids membership of non Catholic unions
Rationing of auto tires ends in US
Second Chamber accept 2nd Police Action in Indonesia
Walter Zinn's Experimental Breeder Reactor I, the first nuclear reactor to produce electric power, goes live at the Argonne National Laboratory, USA
KHQ TV channel 6 in Spokane, WA (NBC) begins broadcasting
KID (now KIDK) TV channel 3 in Idaho Falls, ID (CBS) 1st broadcasting
KWTV TV channel 9 in Oklahoma City, OK (CBS) begins broadcasting
Cardiff is proclaimed the capital city of Wales
Military coup under colonel Simbolon in Sumatra
Montgomery, Alabama removes race-based seat assignments on its buses
Indian cricket spin bowler Jasubhai Patel takes 9-69 in Australia's 1st innings 219 in 2nd Test at Kanpur
Auschwitz commandant Richard Bar arrested in German FR
The National Liberation Front, better known as the Viet Cong, is officially formed in South Vietnam
Berlin Wall opens for 1st time to West Berliners
Massemba-Debate elected President of Congo-Brazzaville
Trial against 21 camp guards of Auschwitz begins
Levi Eshkol forms 12th Israeli government
NBA awards Seattle Supersonics a franchise for 1967-68 season
Nuclear Planning Group forms in Brussels
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
474,300 US soldiers in Vietnam
Ian Anderson and Glenn Cornick form rock group "Jethro Tull" in Blackpool, England
Learie Constantine becomes UK's first person of African descent to be named a life peer (to sit in the House of Lords) in the Crown's New Year's Honours list
The People's Democracy (PD) announce that its members will undertake a protest march from Belfast to Derry beginning 1 January 1969
The Zodiac Killer murders teenagers Betty Lou Jenson and David Faraday while on their first date together in Vallejo, California
Edward Gierek succeeds Wladyslaw Gomulka as Poland's communist party leader
Pakistan president Yahya Khan resigns
Five civilians (four Catholics, one Protestant) killed in gun attack on the Top of the Hill Bar in Derry, North Ireland
Dutch Antillean government of Juancho Evertsz forms
Ethiopia becomes socialist one-party state
Joe Walsh recruited to join Eagles
1st Space walk made by Soviet cosmonaut Georgy Grechko during Salyut 6 EO-1 mission
RAF -terrorist Knut Folkerts sentenced to 20 years

Philadelphia Flyers defenseman Behn Wilson scores with 4:08 remaining in regulation to earn a 1-1 tie with the Pittsburgh Penguins and equal NHL record for longest undefeated streak of 28 games; go on to break record and extend to 35 games
Browns set team records for most fumbles (9) & most turnovers (10)

Winnipeg left wing Doug Smail sets NHL record by scoring just 4 seconds into Jets' 5-4 win over the St Louis Blues; fastest goal from opening face-off to back of net in history; later tied by Bryan Trottier and Alex Mogilny
El Salvador adopts constitution
NY Islanders score their most goals (11) vs Pitts Penguins
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Position of American Poet Laureate established (Robert Warren is 1st)
Sportscaster Howard Cosell retires from television sports after 20 years with ABC
White teenagers beat blacks in New York City's Howard Beach
Dona Paz ferry sinks after crash with oil tanker Vector in Philippine waters, 4,386 die in world's deadliest peacetime maritime disaster
Animal rights terrorists fire-bomb Harrod's department store, London
NBC signs lease to stay in NYC, 33 more years
Premier Ranasinghe Premadasa elected President of Sri Lanka
"Born on the Fourth of July", directed by Oliver Stone and starring Tom Cruise, premieres in America
Premier Lubbers sees CDA-party leader Elco Brinkman as successor
Pentagon warns Saddam that US air power is ready to attack on 1/15
Robert F X Sillerman purchases WAFL NY-NJ Knights for $11 million
The world's first website and server go live at CERN

Missouri court sentences Palestinian militant Zein Isa and his wife Maria to death for the "honor killing" of their teenaged daughter Palestina in St. Louis; he dies of diabetes complications before execution, she dies of natural causes while in prison
NHL approves expansion teams Tampa Lightning & Ottawa Senators
Northwest & KLM introduce a new joint logo "Worldwide Reliability"
American Flight 965 crashes into a mountain in Buga, Colombia, 159 die, 5 survive
NATO begins peacekeeping in Bosnia
First part of the new building for the National Library of France inaugurated in Paris, designed by Dominique Perrault
Portugal returns Macau to China
Argentine economic crisis: President of Argentina Fernando de la Rúa is forced out of office.
US Senator Trent Lott resigns as majority leader.
New York City's Transport Workers Union Local 100 goes on strike over pension and wage increases, shutting down all subway and bus services for three days
The first same sex civil partnerships in Scotland are celebrated
US District Court Judge John E. Jones III rules against mandating the teaching of "intelligent design" in his ruling of Kitzmiller v. Dover Area School District.
A judge rules against the death penalty in the case of Naveed Haq, a man convicted in the shooting death and injuries at the Jewish Federation in Seattle.
Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque completed (begun 1994), becoming the largest mosque in Abu Dhabi
Apple is denied a patent for mobile pinch-to-zoom gestures by the US patent authorities
Intercontinental Exchange purchases the New York Stock Exchange, the largest in the world, for $8 billion
Ismaaiyl Abdullah Brinsley kills 2 NYPD officers in Brooklyn, New York, supposedly in revenge for the killing of Eric Garner, before killing himself
Landslide from construction waste in Shenzhen, China leaves 74 dead or missing and buries 30 buildings
European Commission triggers first step of its article 7 procedure issuing Poland with formal warning of risk to voting rights
US House and Senate pass $1.5 trillion tax and jobs bill, the biggest tax overhaul for decades
New figures show average US male weighs 198 pounds and stands 5 feet 9 inches, women 171 pounds and 5 feet 4 inches
Landmark climate justice case as Dutch Supreme Court upholds 2013 decision brought by Urgenda Foundation against Dutch government for not reducing carbon dioxide emissions
Little Shell Tribe of Chippewa Indians of Montana granted US federal recognition, providing the right to certain legal and government benefits
United States Space Force founded, an armed forces branch dedicated to space warfare
European countries begin closing their borders to the UK after news of a faster-spreading variant of COVID-19 across London and the south-east
First Hong Kong legislative elections with only 'patriot' candidates allowed to stand has low turnout of 30%
6.4-magnitude earthquake near Eureka, northern California kills at least two people
Multinational company 3M says it will stop making and using "forever chemicals", linked to health problems such as cancer
The ruling Taliban suspend university education for female students in Afghanistan, part of a wider crackdown on women's rights in the country
Gaza Health Authority says the death toll has reached nearly 20,000 from the war with Israel - higher than other Arab conflict with Israel in more than 40 years
A 50-year old psychiatrist from Saudi Arabia drives his car through an outdoor Christmas market in Magdeburg, Germany, killing at least five people
Richard the Lionhearted captured near Vienna by Leopold of Austria on his way home from the Third Crusade (released 1194)
Suleiman the Magnificentaccepts surrender of the surviving Knights of Rhodes, who are allowed to evacuate. They eventually settle in Malta and become known as the Knights of Malta.
Prince William of Orange's troops arrive in London
Russian TsarPeter the Greatordered Russian New Year changed from Sept 1 to Jan 1
Bonnie Prince Charlie's Jacobite army reaches the river Esk, North Yorkshire
1st successful US cotton mill begins spinning yarn in Pawtucket, Rhode Island built by Samuel Slater based onRichard Arkwright's design
Play "Rosamunde, Princess of Cyprus" by Helmina von Chézy with incidental music byFranz Schubertpremieres in Vienna
Confederate Army Brigadier GeneralNathan Bedford Forrestoccupies Trenton, Kentucky (US Civil War)
Thomas Edisonprivately demonstrated incandescent light at Menlo Park
Philadelphia Jack O'Brienwins the World Light Heavyweight boxing title after defending championBob Fitzsimmonsof England retires in round 13 of 20 in San Francisco
Soviet state security force and forerunner to the KGB, the Cheka forms, under Felix Dzerzhinsky after decree byVladimir Lenin
Eugene O'Neill's play "Moon of the Caribees" premieres in NYC
Bob Hopebecomes an American citizen aged 17 (emigrated from England aged four)
Adolf Hitlerfreed from jail early, having served only nine months of five-year sentence for "Beer Hall Putsch"
St. Louis Cardinals trade future Baseball Hall of Fame infielderRogers Hornsbyto NY Giants forFrankie Frisch& Jimmy Ring; concerns over Hornsby's gambling
PopePius XIpublishes encyclical Ad Catholici Sacerdotii on the importance of priestly celibacy
Connie Mackacquires controlling interest in the Athletics for $42,000
Terence Rattigan's comedic play "O Mistress Mine" premieres in London
Christmas classic "It's a Wonderful Life" film, directed byFrank Capra, starringJames Stewart, Donna Reed andLionel Barrymorepremieres in New York
Darius Milhaud's 2nd Symphony, premieres with the Boston Symphony conducted by the composer; the work was commissioned as a memorial to the wife of group's music director Serge Koussevitzky
Maurice Raveland John Cranko's ballet "Beauty And The Beast" premieres
"Harvey" starringJames Stewartpremieres in New York
Elvis Presleyreceives his draft notice to join the US Army for national service
American pop singing group the Osmond Brothers debut on "TheAndy WilliamsShow"
"Contempt" film directed byJean-Luc Godard, starringBrigitte Bardotand Jack Palance, is released
Peter, Paul & Mary's cover ofJohn Denver's song "Leaving On A Jet Plane" reaches #1
First preview issue of "Ms." magazine is published in the US launched byGloria Steinem
"The Effect of Gamma Rays on Man-in-the-Moon Marigolds" film directed byPaul Newmanand starring his wife Joanna Woodward, premieres in NYC
AL presidentJoe Croninrefuses to allow Dick Williams to manage Yankees
Montreal CanadienHenri Richardscores his 1,000th NHL point
"TheGodfatherPart II", directed byFrancis Ford Coppolaand starringAl PacinoandRobert De Niro, is released (Academy Awards Best Picture 1975)
George Harrisonreleases "Dark Horse", his fifth studio album in UK
PopePaul VInames Johannes Willebrands Archbishop of Utrecht
Israeli PMYitzhak Rabinresigns
H. R. Haldeman, Nixon's White House Chief of Staff released from jail after serving 18 months
Soviet Union formally announces the death of past PremierAlexei Kosygin
Guy Lafleur, Montreal Canadiens, becomes 10th NHLer to score 500 goals
PLO chairmanYasser Arafatand 4,000 loyalists evacuate Lebanon
Denis Potvinpasses Bobby Orr as NHL defenseman scorer (916 points)
"Working Girl" premieres directed by Mike Nicols, starringMelanie Griffith,Harrison FordandSigourney Weaver
US troops invade Panama & oustManuel Noriega, but don't catch him
Soviet Foreign MinisterEduard Shevardnadzeresigns
"Father of the Bride" film comedy starringSteve Martin,Diane Keaton, andMartin Short, premieres; film is a re-make of 1950 vehicle forSpencer TracyandELizabeth Taylor
Paul Keatingbecomes Prime Minister of Australia after rollingBob Hawkefor leadership of the Labor party
Slobodan Miloševićre-elected president of Serbia
Ballon d'Or: FC Barcelona's Bulgarian forwardHristo Stoichkovis named Europe's best football player ahead of Juventus striker Roberto Baggio and Milan defender Paolo Maldini
Horror film classic "Scream", starringNeve CampbellandCourteney Cox, is released
"Gangs of New York", starringLeonardo DiCaprio,Daniel Day-Lewis, andCameron Diaz, is released
Geffen Records releases "The Breakthrough", the 7th studio album byMary J. Blige; it wins 3 Grammy Awrds, and sells over 3 million copies
Her MajestyQueen Elizabeth IIbecomes the oldest ever monarch of the United Kingdom, surpassing Queen Victoria, who lived for 81 years, 7 months and 29 days.

Painting "Portrait of Suzanne Bloch (1904)", by the Spanish artistPablo Picasso, is stolen from the São Paulo Museum of Art, along with "O Lavrador de Café", by the major Brazilian modernist painter Candido Portinari
Lady Gagasigns Las Vegas residency deal for $75m at MGM Park Theatre
US Defense Secretary Jim Mattis announces his resignation after disagreements with PresidentDonald Trump
Fiji's main opposition parties, led bySitiveni Rabukaform a coalition government, ending 16 years of rule byFrank Bainimarama's Fiji First government
"Grimm's Fairy Tales" or "Children's and Household Tales" by Jacob Grimm andWilhelm Grimmis first published
WWI: ANZAC troops complete their withdrawal from Anzac Cove on the Gallipoli Peninsula
US PresidentBarack Obamareceives his vaccine jab against swine flu
Oronce Finé, French mathematician & cartographer, born in Briançon, France (d. 1555)
John III, King of Sweden (1568-92), born in Östergötland, Sweden (d. 1592)
Paulus Melissus, humanist Neo-Latin writer, translator, and composer, born in Mellrichstadt, Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1602)
Edward Wightman, English radical Anabaptist, born in Burton upon Trent (d. 1612)
Jan Sarkander, Polish-Czech Roman Catholic priest, martyr, and saint, born in Skoczów, Bohemia (now in Poland) (d. 1620)
John Fletcher, English Jacobean playwright (followed Shakespeare as playwright for the Kings Men), born in Rye, England (d. 1625)
Giovanni Battista de Gagliano, Italian composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1651)
Christoph Schultze, German composer, born in Berlin, Margraviate of Brandenburg (d. 1683)
Veit Ludwig von Seckendorff, German statesman, born in Herzogenaurach, Germany (d. 1692)
Pieter de Hoogh, Dutch Golden Age painter, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1684)
François Fagel, Dutch statesman and collector (coins and medals), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1746)
Charles Gravier, comte de Vergennes, French statesman and diplomat, born in Dijon, France (d. 1787)
František Xaver Pokorný Czech violinist and composer, born in Městec Králové, Lands of the Bohemian Crown (d. 1794)
Othon Joseph Vandenbroek, Flemish horn player and composer, born in Ypres, Austrian Netherlands (d. 1832)
Guillaume-Perre-Antoine Gatayes, French composer and guitarist, born in Paris, France (d. 1846)
Pietro Raimondi, Italian composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1853)
Nicolas Charlet, French painter, born in Paris, France (d. 1845)
Richard Lucian Page, US Navy officer (Confederate Army), born in Clarke County, Virginia (d. 1901)
Thomas Tinsley Craven, American naval commander for the Union during the Civil War, born at the Washington Naval Yard (d. 1887)
Friedrich Karl Kuhmstedt, German composer, born in Oldisleben, Grand Duchy of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach (d. 1858)
Achille Peri, Italian composer and conductor, born in Reggio Emilia, Napoleonic Kingdom of Italy (d. 1880)
Samuel Jordan Kirkwood, American politician (Gov-Iowa)/US Sect of Interior (1881-82), born in Harford Country, Maryland (d. 1894)
John Geary, American lawyer and politician (1st SF postmaster; 1st mayor), born in Mount Pleasant, Pennsylvania (d. 1873)
Romeyn Beck Ayres, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Manheim, New York (d. 1888)
Samuel Mudd, American physician (imprisoned for conspiring with John Wilkes Booth), born in Charles Country, Maryland (d. 1883)
Edwin Abbott Abbott, English schoolmaster and theologian (Flatland), born in Marylebone, United Kingdom (d. 1926)
Ferdinand Buisson, French academic and pacifist (Nobel Peace Prize, 1927), born in Paris, France (d. 1932)
Jan Toorop, Dutch-Indonesian painter (3 Brides), born in Purworejo Regency, Indonesia (d. 1928)
Antonius Derkinderen, Dutch painter and artist, born in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands (d. 1925)
Dan Leno [George Wild Galvin], English comedian and stage actor, born in Somers Town, London (d. 1904)
Ivana Kobilca, Slovenian painter, born in Ljubljana, Slovenia (d. 1926)
Elsie De Wolfe, American socialite and interior decorator, born in New York City (d. 1950)
Maud Gonne, English-born Irish revolutionary, born in Tongham, United Kingdom (d. 1953)
Pudge Heffelfinger, American College Football Hall of Fame guard (3 x All American, Yale; first professional player 1892) and coach (Cal, Lehigh, Minnesota), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1954)
Charley Grapewin, American actor (The Wizard of Oz; Libeled Lady), born in Xenia, Ohio (d. 1956)
Henry Kimball Hadley, American composer and conductor, born in Somerville, Massachusetts (d. 1937)
Lorenzo Perosi, Italian priest and sacred oratorio composer, born in Tortona, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1956)
Kan'ichi Asakawa, Japanese historian, writer, and peace activist, born in Nihonmatsu, Fukushima Prefecture, Japan (d. 1948)
T. F. Powys, British novelist and short-story writer (Captain Patch, Goat Green), born in Shirley, Derbyshire (d. 1953)
J. Van Oudshoorn [Jan Koos Feylbrief], Dutch writer (Maze of Senses), born in the Hague, Netherlands (d. 1951)
Walter Sydney Adams, American astronomer (director of Mount Wilson), born in Antioch, Turkey (d. 1956)
Max Blokzijl, Dutch singer, journalist, and Nazi collaborator, born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1946)
Domingo Julio Gómez Garcia, Spanish composer, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1973)
Fred Merkle, American baseball figure, born in Watertown, Wisconsin (d. 1958)
Božidar Širola, Croatian composer, born in Žakanje, Croatia (d. 1956)
Yvonne Arnaud, French-born pianist, singer and actress (Yvonne Arnaud Theatre), born in Bordeaux, Gironde, Aquitaine, France (d. 1958)
Susanne Langer, American philosopher and educator (Philosophy in a New Key), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1985)
John Sparkman, American politician (Sen-D-Alabama 1946-79, Rep-D-Alabama 1937-46), born in Hartselle, Alabama (d. 1985)
Gabby Hartnett, AmericanBaseball Hall of Famecatcher (MLBAll Star 1933–38; NL MVP 1935; Chicago Cubs) and manager (Chicago Cubs), born in Woonsocket, Rhode Island (d. 1972)
Louis Kahn, American architect (Salk Institute), born in Pärnu, Estonia (d. 1974) [or Feb 20]
Robert J. Van de Graaff, American engineer and physicist (Mobility of Gaseous Ions), born in Tuscaloosa, Alabama (d. 1967)
George Edward Alexander Windsor, British nobleman (Duke of Kent), and RAF officer, born in York Cottage, United Kingdom (d. 1942)
Max Lerner, American journalist (NY Post) and educator, born in Minsk, Russia (d. 1992)
Sidney Hook, American philosopher (The Paradoxes of Freedom), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1989)
Hans Burkhardt, Swiss-American abstract artist, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1994)
Albert Dekker, American stage and screen character actor (Dr. Cyclops; Beau Geste; The Wild Bunch), and politician, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1968)
Bill O'Reilly, Australian cricketer, slow-medium leg-spin bowler (144 test wickets at 22.59) and newspaper columnist, born in White Cliffs, Australia (d. 1992)
Dick White, British intelligence officer (Director General of MI5 1953–1956; Head of the Secret Intelligence Service 1956–1968), born in Tonbridge, Kent (d. 1993)
Irving Krick, American meteorologist and inventor, born in San Francisco, California (d. 1996)
Lowell Gilmore, American actor (The Picture of Dorian Gray, Living Christ Series), born in St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 1960)
Paul Francis Webster, American lyricist ("Love Is A Many Splendored Thing"; "The Shadow Of Your Smile"; "I Got It Bad, And That Ain't Good"), born in New York City (d. 1984)
Dennis Morgan, American actor (Kitty Foyle), born in Prentice, Wisconsin (d. 1994)
Francis Turner, American civil engineer (oversaw the Interstate Highway System), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1999)
Julio César Brero [or Giulio Cesare], Italian-born Argentine composer and lawyer (Novella), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1973)
Norman Hackforth, British musician and radio broadcaster (Noel Coward; Twenty Questions), born in Bihar, British India (d. 1996)
Vagn Holmboe, Danish neo-classical composer (Sinfonia rustica), born in Horsens, Jutland (d. 1996)
Vakkom Majeed, Indian freedom fighter and politician, born in the Kingdom of Travancore (d. 2000)
Hortense Calisher, American novelist (President of the American Academy of Arts and Letters), born in New York City (d. 2009)
Jozef Kresánek, Slovak composer and musicologist, born in Čičmany, Kongdom of Hungary (now Slovakia) (d. 1986)
Aziz Nesin, Turkish writer and humorist, born in Heybeliada, Ottoman Empire (d. 1995)
Michel Chartrand, French Canadian union activist, born in Outremont, Quebec (d. 2010)
Audrey Totter, American actress (Carpetbaggers, Set-Up, The Unsuspected), born in Joliet, Illinois (d. 2013)
David Bohm, American physicist, philosopher, and neuropsychologist, born in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania (d. 1992)

George Roy Hill, American Academy Award-winning film director (Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid; The Sting; Slap Shot; A Little Romance; The World According to Garp), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2002)
Charita Bauer, American actress (The Guiding Light, Aldrich Family), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1985)
Denny Vaughan, Canadian pianist, singer, arranger and orchestra leader (The Smothers Brothers Show; Glen Campbell Goodtime Hour), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1972)[1]
Jake Larson, American D-Day veteran and social media star (Story Time with Papa Jake), born in Owatonna, Minnesota (d. 2025)[1]
Errol John, Trinidadian actor and playwright (A Man from the Sun, The Nun's Story, Assault on a Queen), born in Port of Spain, Trinidad (d. 1988)
Judy LaMarsh, Canadian lawyer, politician (Member of Parliament, 1960-68), author, and broadcaster (CBC Radio), born in Chatham-Kent, Ontario (d. 1980)
Benito Lorenzi, Italian footballer, born in Buggiano, Italy (d. 2007)
Bob de Moor, Belgian comics creator and artist (Kuifje, Lion of Flanders), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1992)
Vlastimir Nikolovski, Macedonian composer, born in Prileb, Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (now North Macedonia) (d. 2001)
David Levine, American artist (New York Review of Books caricatures), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2009)
Geoffrey Howe, British politician (Chancellor and Foreign Secretary under Thatcher), born in Aberavon, Glamorganshire (d. 2015)
Otto Graf Lambsdorff, German politician (Free Democratic Party; Minister of Economics, 1977-84), born in Aachen, Germany (d. 2009)
Jim Simpson, American sportscaster, (NBC, ESPN), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 2016)
Kim Young-sam, South Korean politician and democratic activist (President of South Korea 1993-98), born in Geojedo, Geoje, South Korea (d. 2015)
Charles Donald Adams, British bass-baritone opera singer (D'Oyly Carte Opera Company,; Gilbert and Sullivan For All), born in Bristol, United Kingdom (d. 1996)

Jack Christiansen, American NFL football player. 1951-58 (Detroit Lions - 3 x Champion, 5 X Pro Bowl), NFL and college coach (San Francisco 49ers, Stanford University), born in Sublette, Kansas (d. 1986)
Carlo Fassi, Italian figure skating coach (Peggy Fleming, Dorothy Hamill, John Curry, Robin Cousins, Jill Trenary) and skater (World C'ship bronze men's singles 1953), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1997)
Milan Panić, Serbian-American businessman (ICN Pharmaceuticals), politician (Prime Minister of Yugoslavia, 1992-93), and humanitarian, born in Belgrade, Yugoslavia
Mary Ellen "Mala" Powers, American actress (Cyrano de Bergerac, The Wild Wild West), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2007)
John Hillerman, American actor (Magnum PI; Chinatown; Blazing Saddles), born in Denison, Texas (d. 2017)
Neil Armstrong, Canadian ice hockey linesman (1,744 NHL games; Hockey Hall of Fame), born in Plympton, Ontario (d. 2020)
Gordon Getty, American composer, businessman, and heir of J. Paul Getty, born in Los Angeles, California
Jean Carnahan, US politician and writer (Missouri, Senator 2001–2002), born in Washington, D.C.
Rik Van Looy, Belgian cyclist (World Road Race champion 1960, 61; Tour de France Points classification 1963), born in Grobbendonk, Belgium (d. 2024)
Khalid "Billy" Ibadulla, Pakistani cricket batsman (4 Tests; 1 x 100, HS 166; Warwickshire, Otago) and broadcaster (TVNZ), born in Lahore, Pakistan (d. 2024)
Judy Henske, American folk singer (High Flying Bird), born in Chippewa Falls, Wisconsin (d. 2022)
John Harbison, American composer (The Flight Into Egypt; The Great Gatsby), born in Orange, New Jersey
Bill Keith, American bluegrass, country, and session banjoist, and tuning instrument tuner inventor, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2015)
Dianne Arndt, American artist and photographer, born in Springfield, Massachusetts
Kathryn Joosten, American actress (Desperate Housewives), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
Kim Weston, American soul singer (with Marvin Gaye - "It Takes Two"), born in Detroit, Michigan
Klaus Schweizer, German composer, born in Albstadt, Germany
Adolfo Olivares, Chilean soccer striker (15 caps; Ferrobádminton, Universidad de Chile, Huachipato, Santiago Morning), born in Ocoa, Chile (d. 2025)
Larry Willis, American jazz pianist and composer, born in New York City (d. 2019)
Michael Hurley, American folk singer-songwriter ("The Tea Song"), born in Bucks Country, Pennsylvania[1]
Bob Hayes, American athlete (Olympic gold 100m, 4x100m relay 1964) and Pro Football Hall of Fame receiver (3 x Pro Bowl; Dallas Cowboys), born in Jacksonville, Florida (d. 2002)
Héctor Pulido, Mexican soccer midfielder (43 caps; Cruz Azul, Club Jalisco AC), born in Numaran, Mexico (d. 2022)
Angel Tompkins, American actress (Gloria-Search), born in Albany, California
Walter "Wolfman" Washington, American funk, R&B, and blues guitarist (Johnny Adams; The Roadmasters), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2022)
Bobby Colomby, American rock drummer (Blood Sweat & Tears - "Spinning Wheel"), born in New York City
Gernot Wolfgruber, Austrian writer, born in Gmünd, Lower Austria
Jean Fergusson, English actress, born in Wakefield, United Kingdom
Peter Criss [George Peter John Criscuola], American drummer and singer (Kiss - "Beth"), born in Brooklyn, New York
